component.name: button
component.summary: An interactive element that triggers an action when activated.
component.description: An interactive element that triggers an action when activated. Buttons communicate what will happen when the user interacts with them.

anatomy.description: The Button is composed of a container, a text label, and an optional leading or trailing icon.

parts.name: container
parts.description: The outer boundary of the button. Receives background color, border, border radius, and padding. Defines the clickable area.

parts.name: label
parts.description: The text content of the button. Communicates the action that will occur on activation.

parts.name: icon
parts.description: An optional icon displayed before (leading) or after (trailing) the label. Reinforces the label's meaning visually.

parts.name: focus-ring
parts.description: A visible outline rendered when the button receives keyboard focus. Not displayed on mouse interaction.

properties.name: variant
properties.description: The visual style of the button. Determines background color, text color, and border treatment.

presentation.code: High-emphasis — the main action on the surface
Low-emphasis — tertiary actions or dense layouts

properties.name: size
properties.description: The size of the button. Affects padding, font size, icon size, and minimum target area.

presentation.code: Compact density — for toolbars and dense layouts

properties.name: disabled
properties.description: When true, the button is non-interactive. The cursor changes to not-allowed, and the button is visually dimmed to 40% opacity.

properties.name: loading
properties.description: When true, the label is replaced with a spinner and the button is non-interactive. The button retains its dimensions to prevent layout shift.

properties.name: fullWidth
properties.description: When true, the button expands to fill the width of its parent container.

properties.name: iconStart
properties.description: An icon component rendered before the label. When provided without a label, an aria-label is required.

properties.name: iconEnd
properties.description: An icon component rendered after the label.

properties.name: type
properties.description: The HTML button type attribute. Controls form submission behavior.

properties.name: as
properties.description: Renders the button as a different element (e.g., `a` for link-styled buttons). When rendering as an anchor, href is required.

events.name: onClick
events.description: Fires when the button is activated via mouse click, touch tap, Enter key, or Space key. Does not fire when the button is disabled or loading.

events.name: onFocus
events.description: Fires when the button receives focus.

events.name: onBlur
events.description: Fires when the button loses focus.

slots.name: default
slots.acceptedContent: Plain text or a text node. Do not nest interactive elements, headings, or block-level elements.

cssCustomProperties.name: --button-background
cssCustomProperties.description: The background color of the button container.

cssCustomProperties.name: --button-text-color
cssCustomProperties.description: The color of the label text.

cssCustomProperties.name: --button-border-radius
cssCustomProperties.description: The border radius of the button container.

cssCustomProperties.name: --button-padding-horizontal
cssCustomProperties.description: The left and right padding inside the button container.

cssCustomProperties.name: --button-padding-vertical
cssCustomProperties.description: The top and bottom padding inside the button container.

dataAttributes.name: data-state
dataAttributes.description: Reflects the current interactive state of the button. Useful for styling with attribute selectors.

dataAttributes.name: data-variant
dataAttributes.description: Reflects the current variant. Useful for parent-level conditional styling.

variants.name: emphasis
variants.description: Controls the visual weight of the button. Determines background fill, border treatment, and text color to establish a visual hierarchy among actions on a surface.

values.name: primary
values.description: The default, high-emphasis value. Uses a solid, filled background. Use for the single most important action on a surface. Limit to one primary button per surface.

whenToUse.description: When the action is the most important on the surface — the one the user is most likely to take (e.g., Save, Submit, Confirm).
When the surface has a single clear call to action and no competing primary actions.

whenNotToUse.description: When a surface already has a primary button. Adding a second dilutes visual hierarchy.

alternative.name: secondary
alternative.rationale: Secondary emphasis maintains importance without competing with the existing primary action.

values.name: secondary
values.description: A medium-emphasis value with a visible border and transparent background. Use for actions that are important but not the primary action on the surface.

whenToUse.description: When the action is important but secondary to a primary action on the same surface (e.g., Cancel alongside Save).
When multiple actions of equal importance appear together and none should dominate.

values.name: ghost
values.description: A low-emphasis value with no background or border in its default state. Use for tertiary actions, toolbar actions, or in dense layouts where visual weight must be minimized.

whenToUse.description: When the action is tertiary or supplementary — helpful but not essential to the user's primary task.
When the button appears in a dense layout such as a toolbar, table row, or card footer where visual weight must be minimized.

whenNotToUse.description: When the action is the only action on the surface and needs to be clearly discoverable.

alternative.name: secondary
alternative.rationale: A ghost button on its own can be overlooked. Secondary emphasis provides enough visual presence to be discoverable without dominating the layout.

values.name: danger
values.description: A high-emphasis value that signals a destructive or irreversible action. Use for delete, remove, or disconnect actions. Pair with a confirmation dialog for irreversible operations.

whenToUse.description: When the action is destructive or irreversible — deleting a record, revoking access, removing a team member.

whenNotToUse.description: When the action is not destructive, even if it feels important or urgent.

alternative.name: primary
alternative.rationale: The danger color is a strong signal reserved for destruction. Using it for non-destructive actions dilutes its meaning and conditions users to ignore it.

variants.name: size
variants.description: Controls the physical dimensions and internal padding of the button. All sizes maintain a minimum 44×44 CSS pixel tap target.

values.name: small
values.description: Compact size for toolbars, table rows, and dense layouts. Visible area is smaller but internal padding extends the tap target to 44×44px.

whenToUse.description: When the button appears in a space-constrained context such as a toolbar, data table row, or inline action.

whenNotToUse.description: When the button is a primary page-level action that needs to be prominent and easy to tap on mobile.

alternative.name: medium
alternative.rationale: Medium is the default size and provides a larger visible target, making it easier to discover and activate.

values.name: medium
values.description: The default size. Suitable for most contexts including forms, dialogs, and page-level actions.

whenToUse.description: For most buttons across the system — forms, dialogs, page actions, and cards.

values.name: large
values.description: Touch-optimized size for mobile-first surfaces, hero sections, and marketing pages. Provides a larger visible tap target.

whenToUse.description: When the button appears on a mobile-first surface, a marketing hero, or a context where the action must be unmissable.

whenNotToUse.description: When the button appears in a dense layout alongside many other controls.

alternative.name: medium
alternative.rationale: Large buttons in dense layouts consume disproportionate space and disrupt visual rhythm.

variants.name: full-width
variants.description: When active, the button stretches to fill the full width of its parent container. A single-value variant — the button is either full-width or it is not.

values.name: full-width
values.description: The button expands to 100% of its container width. Label text is centered.

whenToUse.description: When the button is the sole action in a narrow container such as a mobile sheet, a card footer, or a single-column form.

whenNotToUse.description: When multiple buttons appear side by side in a button group or dialog footer.

alternative.name: default width
alternative.rationale: Full-width buttons in a horizontal group force a stacked layout, which breaks the expected left-to-right reading order for action groups.

variants.name: icon-only
variants.description: When active, the button displays only an icon with no visible text label. Requires an aria-label for accessibility.

values.name: icon-only
values.description: The button renders a single icon with no visible label. The aria-label attribute is required to provide an accessible name.

whenToUse.description: When the action is universally understood by its icon alone (e.g., close ✕, search 🔍, menu ☰) and space is constrained.

whenNotToUse.description: When the action is ambiguous or unfamiliar to the target audience without a text label.

alternative.name: default (icon + label)
alternative.rationale: Icons are not universally understood. When there is room for a text label, include one. Research consistently shows that icon-plus-label buttons are faster to identify and less error-prone than icon-only buttons.

states.name: default
states.description: The button's resting state when no interaction is occurring.

states.name: hover
states.description: Triggered when the user's pointer moves over the button. The background darkens by 8% to indicate interactivity. Not applicable on touch devices.

states.name: active
states.description: Triggered while the button is being pressed (mousedown or touch start). The background darkens by 16% from the default to indicate activation.

states.name: focus
states.description: Triggered when the button receives keyboard focus. A 2px focus ring appears with a 2px offset from the container edge. The focus ring uses the system focus color.

states.name: disabled
states.description: The button is non-interactive. Opacity is reduced to 0.4. Pointer events are disabled. The button remains in the tab order when using aria-disabled instead of the HTML disabled attribute.

states.name: loading
states.description: The button label is replaced by a spinner animation. The button is non-interactive. The button maintains its dimensions from the default state to prevent layout shift.

responsive.description: When fullWidth is not set, buttons retain their intrinsic width. In narrow containers (below 320px), buttons expand to full width automatically to maintain a usable tap target.
Buttons display at their intrinsic width. Button groups display inline.

whenToUse.description: When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form, saving data, opening a dialog, or confirming a decision.
When the user needs to dismiss or cancel an in-progress workflow, such as closing a modal or discarding unsaved changes.
When the interface requires a toggle action that changes state without navigating away, such as expanding a section or enabling a feature.
When a destructive or irreversible action needs to be initiated, such as deleting a record or revoking access. Pair with a confirmation dialog.

whenNotToUse.description: When the action navigates the user to a different page or URL.

alternative.name: link
alternative.rationale: Links carry native navigation semantics. Screen readers announce them as links, and browsers support standard navigation behaviors such as open-in-new-tab. Using a button for navigation creates a mismatch between the announced role and the actual behavior.

whenNotToUse.description: When the user needs to select one option from a set of mutually exclusive choices.

alternative.name: radio-group
alternative.rationale: Radio groups communicate exclusivity through their semantic role. A set of buttons styled to look like a selector does not convey mutual exclusivity to assistive technology.

whenNotToUse.description: When the action reveals a list of additional actions or navigation items.

alternative.name: menu-button
alternative.rationale: Menu buttons combine a trigger with a disclosure pattern. They manage focus into the opened menu and announce the expanded state. A plain button requires manual implementation of these behaviors.

whenNotToUse.description: When the control toggles between two states and the current state must be visually persistent (e.g., bold on/off in a toolbar).

alternative.name: toggle-button
alternative.rationale: Toggle buttons use aria-pressed to communicate the current state. A standard button does not convey that it represents a persistent on/off value.

whenNotToUse.description: When the only content is an icon with no visible text label.

alternative.name: icon-button
alternative.rationale: Icon buttons enforce an aria-label requirement and apply size adjustments for icon-only touch targets. A standard button with its label removed may fail accessibility requirements silently.

guidelines.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary button.
guidelines.rationale: Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. A single primary button directs the user to the most important action.

examples.description: One primary action with a secondary alternative.

presentation.alt: A dialog footer with a secondary Cancel button on the left and a primary Save button on the right. Only the Save button has a filled blue background.

examples.description: Two primary buttons create competing focal points.

examples.title: Multiple primary buttons competing for attention

presentation.alt: A dialog footer with two primary buttons, both with filled blue backgrounds. Cancel and Save compete for visual attention.

guidelines.guidance: Place the primary button on the right side of a button group in left-to-right layouts. Place it on the left in right-to-left layouts.
guidelines.rationale: Users scan in the direction of the layout's reading order. Placing the primary action at the natural endpoint aligns with the completion point of reading. This convention is also established by operating system dialog patterns.

presentation.alt: A dialog footer in a left-to-right layout. The secondary Cancel button is on the left, the primary Save button is on the right.

examples.title: Primary button on the left in LTR layout

presentation.alt: A dialog footer in a left-to-right layout where the primary Save button is on the left and the secondary Cancel button is on the right, reversing the expected order.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use a Button when the action navigates the user to a different page or URL. Use a Link component instead.
guidelines.rationale: Buttons and links have different semantic roles. Buttons trigger actions (submit, open, close). Links navigate. Screen reader users rely on element role to anticipate behavior. Using a button for navigation creates a mismatch between the announced role and the actual behavior.

guidelines.guidance: Use the danger variant exclusively for destructive or irreversible actions. Pair danger buttons with a confirmation dialog.
guidelines.rationale: Red is a strong signal. If danger styling is used for non-destructive actions, it dilutes the warning signal and conditions users to ignore it. A confirmation dialog prevents accidental data loss.

examples.title: Danger button paired with confirmation dialog

presentation.alt: A confirmation dialog titled 'Delete project?' with body text explaining the action is permanent. The footer contains a secondary Cancel button and a danger Delete project button with a red background.

presentation.code:
<ConfirmDialog
  title="Delete project?"
  description="This action is permanent and cannot be undone."
  onConfirm={handleDelete}
>
  <Button variant="danger">Delete project</Button>
</ConfirmDialog>

examples.title: Danger variant misused for a non-destructive action

presentation.alt: A settings form where the Submit button uses the danger variant with a red background, even though the action is not destructive.

guidelines.guidance: Do not disable a button without indicating to the user what action is needed to enable it.
guidelines.rationale: A disabled button with no explanation leaves the user stuck. They cannot tell what prerequisite they have not met. Pair disabled buttons with helper text, a tooltip, or inline validation that explains the condition.

presentation.alt: A disabled Submit button below a form. Inline validation text reads 'Complete all required fields to continue.' The required fields are highlighted with red borders.

presentation.code:
<>
  <Button variant="primary" aria-disabled={!isFormValid}>
    Submit
  </Button>
  {!isFormValid && (
    <HelperText>Complete all required fields to continue.</HelperText>
  )}
</>

presentation.alt: A disabled Submit button below a form with no visible indication of what the user must do to enable it. The button appears at 40% opacity with no accompanying text.

guidelines.guidance: Use the loading state instead of disabling the button during asynchronous operations.
guidelines.rationale: A disabled button gives no feedback that an action is in progress. The loading state communicates that the action was registered and the system is working. It also prevents double-submission.

presentation.alt: A user clicks a primary button labeled 'Save'. The label is replaced by a spinner animation while the button maintains its width. After two seconds, the spinner is replaced by a checkmark and the label changes to 'Saved'.

examples.title: Disabled state during async — no user feedback

guidelines.guidance: Do not wrap a button's label text across multiple lines.
guidelines.rationale: Multi-line button labels are harder to scan and create inconsistent button heights in groups. If the label is too long, rewrite it to be shorter.

examples.title: Single-line labels with consistent button height

presentation.alt: Three buttons in a row, each with a short single-line label: 'Save', 'Export', and 'Share'. All buttons have consistent height.

examples.title: Multi-line label creates uneven button height

presentation.alt: Two buttons in a row. The first button has the label 'Save' on one line. The second button has the label 'Export and download all items' wrapped across two lines, making it taller than the first button.

guidelines.guidance: Maintain a minimum tap target of 44x44 CSS pixels for all button sizes.
guidelines.rationale: The WCAG 2.5.8 target size criterion requires a minimum 24x24px target, with 44x44px recommended. Touch devices require larger targets to prevent mis-taps. The small button size uses internal padding to meet this requirement even when the visible area is compact.

examples.title: Tap target meets 44×44px via internal padding

presentation.alt: A small button labeled 'Edit' with a dotted overlay showing the 44x44 pixel tap target area. The visible button is smaller, but internal padding extends the interactive region to the minimum target size.
A small icon-only button with a dotted overlay showing a 28x28 pixel tap target area, below the required 44x44 pixel minimum.

guidelines.guidance: Use a verb or verb phrase that describes the action the button performs. Two words maximum.
guidelines.rationale: Action-oriented labels set clear expectations about what will happen on activation. Short labels prevent truncation on narrow viewports and improve scannability in dense interfaces.
Sentence case is easier to read than title case or all caps. It also localizes more predictably across languages where capitalization rules differ.

guidelines.guidance: Do not end the label with punctuation.
guidelines.rationale: Button labels are not sentences. Punctuation adds visual noise without conveying meaning.

guidelines.guidance: In a confirmation dialog, the button label must match the action described in the dialog's message. Do not use generic labels like 'OK' or 'Yes'.
guidelines.rationale: Generic labels force the user to re-read the dialog message to understand what the button will do. Specific labels like 'Delete account' remove ambiguity and reduce the chance of accidental confirmation.

examples.description: The button label mirrors the action described in the dialog.

examples.title: Button label matches the dialog action

presentation.alt: A confirmation dialog titled 'Delete this project?' with body text 'This action is permanent.' The footer has a secondary Cancel button and a danger button labeled 'Delete project' that matches the dialog title.

examples.description: 'OK' does not communicate which action will occur.

presentation.alt: A confirmation dialog titled 'Delete this project?' with body text 'This action is permanent.' The footer has a secondary Cancel button and a primary button labeled 'OK', which does not indicate what action will occur.

guidelines.guidance: When using an icon-only button (no visible label), provide an aria-label that describes the action.
guidelines.rationale: Screen readers announce button content as the accessible name. Without visible text, there is no accessible name. The aria-label provides one.

guidelines.guidance: Use the native <button> element. Do not recreate button behavior on a <div> or <span>.
guidelines.rationale: Native buttons provide built-in keyboard interaction (Enter, Space), focus management, and form submission behavior. Recreating this on a non-semantic element requires manual ARIA roles, keyboard event handlers, and is error-prone.

guidelines.guidance: Prefer aria-disabled="true" over the HTML disabled attribute when the button should remain discoverable by screen reader users.
guidelines.rationale: The HTML disabled attribute removes the button from the tab order, making it invisible to keyboard users. aria-disabled keeps the button focusable and announceable while communicating that it is inactive.

guidelines.guidance: The focus ring must be visible in all color modes (light, dark, high contrast).
guidelines.rationale: Keyboard users depend on the focus indicator to track their position. If the focus ring is invisible against the background, navigation becomes impossible.

guidelines.guidance: Button label text must meet a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against the button background. The button background must meet a minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against the surrounding surface.
guidelines.rationale: Text contrast ensures readability for users with low vision. Non-text contrast ensures the button boundary is perceivable.

keyboardInteraction.action: Moves focus to the next focusable element in the tab order.
Moves focus to the previous focusable element in the tab order.

ariaAttributes.description: Applied automatically by the <button> element. Only set explicitly when using the 'as' prop to render a non-button element (e.g., an anchor).
Set to 'true' when the button is non-interactive. Preferred over the HTML disabled attribute when the button should remain focusable for screen reader discoverability.
Provides an accessible name for icon-only buttons that lack visible text. Not needed when a visible label is present.
Set to 'true' when the button is in the loading state. Communicates to assistive technology that the button's action is in progress.

accessibility.screenReaderBehavior: Announced as '[label], button'. When disabled via aria-disabled, announced as '[label], button, dimmed' (VoiceOver) or '[label], button, unavailable' (NVDA/JAWS). When loading, announced as '[label], button, busy'.
accessibility.focusManagement: The button participates in the normal tab order. It does not trap or redirect focus. When the button triggers a modal or popover, focus is moved to the opened element — this is the responsibility of the modal/popover component, not the button.

colorContrast.context: Label text on primary button background in light mode.
Label text on primary button background in dark mode.
Secondary button border/text against the default page background in light mode.
Label text on danger button background in light mode.
